ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai olive fried rice; mapped to the closest parent class 'fried rice food product' (FOODON_00002678) as the most defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FAN (Fried rice) used as the closest parent category; no specific code exists for Thai olive fried rice variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; not clinically coded as a distinct entity.

main_ingredients: preserved Chinese olives (Thai olives / Canarium album)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Thai olive fried rice weighs approximately 350 g, providing about 651 kcal, 14 g protein, 21 g fat, and 101 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Olive Fried Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Thai olive fried rice (cr_id F05100145, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were not available in HPB and were estimated from comparable fried rice preparations; these fields carry reduced confidence (0.7). Sugar value of -1.0 in source data is a recording sentinel indicating data not available; treated as -1 in output with documentation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database — White rice and fried rice analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai olive fried rice. GI of 75 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based fried rice (GI ~72–80, mean ~75). Dietary fibre at 3.3 g/100g may modestly reduce effective GI; however, the dominant ingredient is cooked white/mixed rice and the high-GI classification is retained. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 100.8) / 100 = 75.6. Carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 28.8 × 3.5 = 100.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 335 mg/100g from preserved olives and soy sauce; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1173 mg sodium, representing 51% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced soy sauce and salt during preparation, as the preserved olive and soy sauce content makes this dish borderline unsuitable at standard serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (75) and high glycaemic load per serving (75.6) due to rice base; partially offset by dietary fibre at 3.3 g/100g which moderates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portions to 150–200 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables or protein to lower the overall glycaemic response, and monitor blood glucose after consumption given the high glycaemic load at standard serving sizes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium content (335 mg/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at 0.86 g/100g and trans fat is 0 g/100g, which are favourable; dietary fibre at 3.3 g/100g provides a modest cardioprotective benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish is acceptable in small portions (150 g) with reduced seasoning; the low saturated fat and zero trans fat profile are positive, but the high sodium from preserved olives and soy sauce requires careful portion management to stay within the 1500–2000 mg/day sodium target.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients including olives (MUFA, polyphenols), kale (sulforaphane, vitamin C 8.57 mg/100g), and garlic; low saturated fat (0.86 g/100g) and zero trans fat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Thai olive fried rice is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory ingredient profile; use reduced-sodium soy sauce to minimise sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai olive fried rice is a Singaporean hawker dish featuring cooked rice stir-fried in a hot wok with preserved Chinese olives (Canarium album, known locally as 'Thai olives'), egg, garlic, kale, soy sauce, and vegetable oil. The preserved olives impart a distinctive briny, slightly bitter, and umami-rich flavour that differentiates this dish from standard egg fried rice. The texture is fluffy with slightly chewy olive pieces and tender kale. Per 100 g, the dish provides 186 kcal, 4 g protein, 6.07 g fat (saturated fat 0.86 g, trans fat 0 g), 28.8 g carbohydrate, 3.3 g dietary fibre, and 335 mg sodium. The high dietary fibre content (3.3 g/100g) from the olive and kale components is nutritionally notable. The dish has a high glycaemic index (~75) due to its rice base, and a standard 350 g plate delivers a glycaemic load of approximately 75.6, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium is elevated at 335 mg/100g primarily from preserved olives and soy sauce, requiring caution for hypertensive individuals. The dish is commonly found in Singapore school canteens and hawker centres and is classified as a high-fibre rice dish. It is not suitable for strict halal diets without verified halal-certified ingredients.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id F05100145, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, vitamin C) are from a supplementary source with reduced confidence (0.7). Sugar value is a sentinel -1 (data not available in source). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice-based fried rice. Overall record confidence is high given the primary HPB source match.
notes: 1. sugar_g is set to -1.0: the HPB source record contains a sentinel value of -1.0 for sugar, indicating that sugar data was not measured or reported for this specific preparation. This is not a data error in the output — it is a documented data gap. Treat as data not available rather than 0 g/100g for clinical purposes. Source consulted: HPB Foodo database (F05100145) confirmed no sugar value reported. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Foodo for this dish. USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact match for Thai olive fried rice; fatty acid sub-fractions not estimable with sufficient confidence. 3. potassium_mg set to -1.0: not reported in HPB source; estimated ~180 mg/100g from ingredient composition but not confirmed by an approved source, so -1.0 is used. 4. All remaining micronutrients set to -1.0 are not reported in the HPB source and no approved-source equivalent was available for estimation. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this vegetable-oil-based stir-fried preparation per HPB source data. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: reported as 0 in the supplementary grabfood-derived data; plausible given egg content is modest, but confidence is 0.7. Treat as approximate. 7. Glycaemic load per serving recalculated: GI (75) × carbohydrate per 350 g serving (100.8 g) / 100 = 75.6. Input record stated GL of 21.6 which appears to have been calculated on a 100 g basis (75 × 28.8 / 100 = 21.6); the per-serving GL of 75.6 is used in this output as the template requires GL per serving. 8. is_halal set to false as preserved olives and soy sauce sourcing cannot be confirmed halal without product-level verification. 9. Dish is commonly served in Singapore school canteens and hawker centres; classified as a Singaporean-Thai fusion hawker dish. 10. ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'broader') to conform to the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.
